title: Ash serving tray with cut-out handles
subtitle: A flat ash tray with integral cut-out handles at each end
excerpt: A flat ash serving tray 450 mm by 320 mm with 100 mm oval cut-out handles at each short end. The tray is cut from a single 18 mm ash board; the handles are cut out with a coping saw and finished with a chisel and spokeshave. Ash's open grain is sealed and oiled to a smooth food-safe finish.
sourceNotes: Victorian kitchen and domestic craft manuals for wooden trays and salvers; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885).

glossaryTerms.definition: A small two-handled plane for shaping and smoothing curves. The flat-bottomed spokeshave follows convex curves; the round-bottomed variant works concave surfaces. Used here to smooth the inside of the cut-out handles after coping-saw work.

Applying a sanding sealer or fine paste filler to open-grained hardwoods (ash, oak) before the final coat of oil or finish. Ash has a coarse open grain that traps food particles if left unsealed on a serving surface.

content.text: Keep both hands behind the cutting edge. Clamp the tray blank to the bench before coping-saw or chisel work.

A flat tray without raised sides requires no joinery; the difficulty is in achieving a surface flat and smooth enough to hold a glass without rocking, and in shaping the cut-out handles so they are comfortable and clean-edged. The
does the work inside the handle opening that a plane cannot reach.
One seasoned ash board 450 mm by 320 mm by 18 mm. Sanding sealer and food-grade oil for finishing.

Saw the tray blank to size and square all four edges. Mark the handle opening at each short end: an oval 100 mm by 45 mm, centred, 30 mm from the end edge and 30 mm from each long side. Drill a 12 mm starter hole inside the marked oval, then cut around the outline with a coping saw.

The coping saw leaves a rough edge. Pare the long sides of the oval with a bevel-edge chisel, then work the curved ends with the flat-bottomed spokeshave, taking light cuts with the grain. The inside surface should be smooth and free of saw marks before finishing. Ease all edges with a light chamfer using a chisel or a few passes with 120-grit sandpaper.

Plane the top face flat with the smoothing plane, working diagonally then with the grain. Finish with a card scraper. Ash is open-grained; apply one coat of sanding sealer and rub back with 320-grit paper before oiling, to achieve the
that makes the surface food-safe and easy to wipe clean. Apply two coats of food-grade oil after sealing.

items.symptom: Handle edge feels rough under the hand even after spokeshave work
items.cause: Spokeshave taken against the grain on the curved end of the handle; fibres torn rather than cut
items.fix: Reverse direction at the midpoint of each curved end: approach the tight curve from one side, then the other. The spokeshave cuts cleanly only when moving with the grain.
